title: Manipulation of the gyrotropic mode frequency and band structure in an FM / AFM disk via vortex imprinting
authors: E. V. Skorokhodov, E. A. Karashtin, I. A. Fedotov, I. Yu. Pashen'kin, M. V. Sapozhnikov
abstract: We experimentally investigate the magnetic gyrotropic mode in a system of vortex ferromagnetic (FM) nanooscillator exchange coupled to an antiferromagnetic (AFM) layer. The micron-sized disks formed from the Ni80Fe20(12 nm) / Ir80Mn20 (5 nm) FM/AFM heterostructure are prepared so that the vortex magnetic state is imprinted into the AFM layer. We apply a magnetic resonance force microscopy (MRFM) method to locally study magnetic oscillations in single FM/AFM disks. We show that the gyrotropic mode frequency is significantly (approximately 4 times) shifted to the high frequency compared to similar structure consisting of a single ferromagnetic disk. Upon applying an in-plane magnetic field, we observe a strong peak at a double frequency which was previously predicted in theory. This is governed by the fact that vortex dynamics is strongly nonlinear in a noncentrosymmetric system under investigation.
summary: This work experimentally and theoretically investigates how coupling a Ferromagnetic layer to an Antiferromagnetic layer via vortex imprinting modifies the system's fundamental magnetic resonance frequency. Researchers report a significant frequency shift and observe a predicted double-frequency mode when applying an external field, confirming strong nonlinear dynamics governed by interfacial exchange coupling.
main_problem: The study investigates the manipulation of the magnetic gyrotropic mode frequency and band structure within a Ferromagnetic (FM) / Antiferromagnetic (AFM) disk system.
main_result: The coupling between FM and AFM layers via vortex imprinting causes a significant shift in the gyrotropic mode frequency, and the application of an in-plane field reveals a predicted double-frequency resonance due to nonlinear dynamics.
method: The research combines experimental measurements using Magnetic Resonance Force Microscopy (MRFM) with theoretical modeling using micromagnetic simulations.
model_or_system: The physical system is an FM/AFM bilayer disk heterostructure, such as NiFe/IrMn. The AFM layer's vortex state is imprinted into the material via controlled thermal annealing processes.
key_observables: Gyrotropic mode frequency ($\omega_{	ext{exch}}$), resonance spectra (comparing single FM vs. FM/AFM disks), and the dependence of these frequencies on an applied in-plane magnetic field ($H$).
important_parameters: The frequency shift is proportional to exchange coupling terms, and the system exhibits strong nonlinearity when subjected to external fields.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the observed frequency shifts and nonlinearities are governed by the surface exchange interaction between the FM and AFM vortices in a noncentrosymmetric environment.
figures_summary: Figures compare MRFM spectra showing the large frequency shift (single vs. bilayer) and demonstrate the emergence of a distinct double-frequency peak when an external magnetic field is applied to the coupled system.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the physical setup and characterization methods (MOKE, MRFM), presenting experimental measurements showing the frequency shift and nonlinear response, and then supporting these findings with theoretical micromagnetic calculations that predict the double-frequency mode.
why_interesting: While focused on magnetism, the study of highly nonlinear dynamics, symmetry breaking (noncentrosymmetry), and resonant modes in coupled magnetic systems shares conceptual overlap with understanding collective excitations in quantum many-body or open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The illustration of the exchange energy growth due to the FM vortex (red arrows) shift with respect to the AFM vortex (blue arrows show the magnetic moment of the surface layer).
Figure 2: Scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of a fragment of the disk array. The disk diameter and the distance between edges are 1 µm ± 10nm.

Figure 3: Magnetization curves of an array of disks made of a Ta(20 nm)/IrMn(5 nm)/Fe(0.5 nm)/NiFe(12 nm)/Ta(5 nm) film (a) before annealing and (b) after annealing at 350◦C and subsequent zero-field cooling.
Figure 5 shows the ferromagnetic resonance spectra obtained by magnetic resonance
Figure 4: MOKE magnetization curve of a micron-sized disk array made of a Fe(0.5 nm)/NiFe(12 nm)/Ta(5 nm) film.
